chunks.text: Daniel Klein is a Professor at EECS, UC Berkeley. Contact information: 8058 Berkeley Way West, tel: 510-643-0805, fax: 510-642-5775, email: klein@cs.berkeley.edu. Office hours: Tuesday 2pm-3:30pm (may be in 778 SDH), 730 Sutardja Dai. Links include Personal Homepage, Publications, Dissertations, and Research Support. Leslie Goldstein: 765 Soda, 3-2469, lgolds@berkeley.edu.

Professor Klein's research focuses on statistical natural language processing, including unsupervised learning methods, syntactic parsing, information extraction, and machine translation. For specific projects and publications, see his group's webpage. He received his BA in Math, CS, Linguistics (summa cum laude) from Cornell University (1994-1998); an M.St. in Linguistics from Oxford University (1998-1999); and an M.S. and Ph.D. in Computer Science from Stanford University (1999-2004).

Recent honors and awards include the ACM's Grace Murray Hopper Award, the Sloan Research Fellowship, the NSF CAREER Award, and the Microsoft New Faculty Fellowship. He was awarded the Computer Science Division's Jim and Donna Gray Award for Excellence in Undergraduate Teaching in 2009, and UC Berkeley's Distinguished Teaching Award in 2010, and the Diane S. McEntyre Award for Excellence in Teaching in 2011. He has won best paper awards with co-authors at NAACL 2010 for 'Coreference Resolution in a Modular, Entity-Centered Model,' at ACL 2009 for 'K-Best A* Parsing,' at NAACL 2006 for 'Prototype-Driven Sequence Learning,' EMNLP 2004 for 'Max-Margin Parsing,' and at ACL 2003 for 'Accurate Unlexicalized Parsing.'

Education: 2004, M.S./Ph.D., Computer Science, Stanford University; 1999, M.St, Linguistics, Oxford University; 1998, B.A., Math, CS, Linguistics, Cornell University. Research Areas: Artificial Intelligence (AI), Natural Language Processing, Computational Linguistics, Machine Learning. Research Centers: Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research Lab (BAIR). Teaching Schedule: CS 188. Introduction to Artificial Intelligence, TuTh 12:30-13:59, Lewis 100.

Selected Publications include works on tree annotation, machine translation, sequence models, word alignment, unsupervised learning, max-margin parsing, corpus-based induction, A* parsing, unlexicalized parsing, and generative models. These were published in conferences such as CoLing-ACL, HLT-NAACL, EMNLP, and ACL between 2002 and 2006, with several receiving best paper awards.

Selected Honors and Awards: Bakar Fellows Spark Award (2022), Diane S. McEntyre Award for Excellence in Teaching Computer Science (2011), UC Berkeley Distinguished Teaching Award (2010), Okawa Research Grant (2009), Jim and Donna Gray Award for Excellence in Undergraduate Teaching of Computer Science (2009), Sloan Research Fellow (2007), Hellman Fellow (2006), Grace Murray Hopper Award (2006), Microsoft New Faculty Fellow (2004).
